text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Helen Carter  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, Queen Square, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Helen Carter,  

James Rutherford, DOB: 22-09-1984, Hospital No: U918274 NHS No. 4082365197  
Flat 12, 6 Anchor Wharf, Aberdeen, AB11 5DL 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy with prior head trauma (remote)/history of migraine without aura/vitamin D insufficiency corrected in 2024/works offshore on rotational schedule/no current alcohol use/non-smoker. 

Medication: Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily.  
Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily.  
Cholecalciferol 1000 units once daily.  
Omeprazole 20 mg once daily as needed for dyspepsia.  

I conducted a scheduled telephone review with him today while he is onshore between offshore rotations. He reports feeling well overall. Sleep is regular when offshore due to fixed shifts and he maintains hydration and meal timing. He has identified that prolonged sleep deprivation previously made him feel cognitively slower, and he now uses earplugs and light-blocking shades on the rig to maintain sleep routine. He denies mood symptoms. No new neurological complaints, headaches are infrequent and responsive to simple analgesia. 

Seizures: He and his employer’s occupational health records indicate no events since early 2023. He describes continued stability without episodes of loss of awareness, convulsions, or auras, and no missed doses. This pattern has persisted on his present antiseizure medicines. He carries rescue instructions but has not needed them. Offshore medical staff are aware of his condition and hold a copy of his care plan. 

Work/Function: Offshore rotation is typically 3 weeks on/3 weeks off. Telephone reviews are arranged for onshore periods; he is happy to attend face-to-face annually or sooner if concerns arise. He avoids high-risk tasks during any periods of ill health and adheres to employer safety protocols. 

Examination: Not examined today due to telephone format. Weight last recorded by occupational health in August 2025: 82 kg; BP 126/78 mmHg. No interim injuries reported. 

Investigations: Routine bloods via GP in August 2025 reported normal FBC, U&Es, LFTs; vitamin D 78 nmol/L. ECG January 2025 normal sinus rhythm. No recent imaging required. 

Plan:  
- Continue current antiseizure medication without change.  
- Maintain sleep hygiene and hydration during offshore rotations.  
- Annual safety letter for employer/occupational health to be updated; patient to share copy.  
- Telephone follow-up in 6 months when onshore, with earlier contact if any events, medication issues, or adverse effects.  
- GP to recheck routine bloods in 12 months or earlier if clinically indicated. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly addresses seizures: "no events since early 2023" and "continued stability without episodes of loss of awareness, convulsions, or auras." Clinic date is 02 October 2025. From early 2023 to October 2025 is well over 6 months (approximately 1 year 9 months or more). Therefore, the patient is currently seizure free and has been so for multiple years. Per the rules, when seizure free for at least 6 months, we can label as seizure free for multiple years since exact year count is not specified to a precise integer. No higher frequency is reported; thus use seizure free for multiple years.

flaws.description: Gold (seizure free for multiple year) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
